linked_sections.paper_title: Polymer informatics at-scale with multitask graph neural networks
linked_sections.heading: Summary and Outlook
linked_sections.text_snippet: In summary, we have produced polyGNN-the first-ever protocol that integrates polymer feature learning from SMILES strings and other relevant features, invariant transformations, data augmentation and multitask learning. Through careful comparison, we show that our protocol culminates in ultrafast polymer fingerprinting and accurate property prediction over the most comprehensive array of chemistries and properties studied to date.

papers.title: Constrained Bayesian Optimization for Automatic Chemical Design
papers.abstract: Automatic Chemical Design is a framework for generating novel molecules with optimized properties. The original scheme, featuring Bayesian optimization over the latent space of a variational autoencoder, suffers from the pathology that it tends to produce invalid molecular structures. First, we demonstrate empirically that this pathology arises when the Bayesian optimization scheme queries latent points far away from the data on which the variational autoencoder has been trained. Secondly, by reformulating the search procedure as a constrained Bayesian optimization problem, we show that the effects of this pathology can be mitigated, yielding marked improvements in the validity of the generated molecules. We posit that constrained Bayesian optimization is a good approach for solving this class of training set mismatch in many generative tasks involving Bayesian optimization over the latent space of a variational autoencoder.

While the specific target property (dielectric constant vs. PCE) and molecule type (polymers vs. general molecules/materials) differ, the core problem of using VAEs and Bayesian optimization for material property optimization is very similar. | Method: The paper's method is very similar to the user's idea. Both apply a variational autoencoder to encode molecular representations into a continuous latent space and then use Bayesian optimization in that space to search for molecules with optimized properties. The key difference is the paper's addition of 'constrained Bayesian optimization' to mitigate invalid molecular structures, which is an incremental improvement on the core methodology shared with the user's idea. | Domain: The user's idea focuses on 'polymers with high dielectric constants', which falls under materials design. The paper explicitly includes 'Experiment II: Material Design' where it trains a model on data from the 'Harvard Clean Energy Project' to generate 'molecules optimized for power conversion efficiency (PCE)'. While the specific target property (dielectric constant vs. PCE) differs, both are within the domain of materials design for energy applications, making them closely related application targets within the same sub-area. | Contribution: The user's research idea proposes applying a variational autoencoder to encode molecular fingerprints of polymer repeat units into a continuous latent space and then using Bayesian optimization to search for polymers with high dielectric constants. The paper explicitly demonstrates this by training a model on data from the Harvard Clean Energy Project to generate molecules optimized for power conversion efficiency (PCE) using 512-bit Morgan circular fingerprints as input features (Experiment II: Material Design, PCE Scores, Implementation). This directly aligns with the user's proposed methodology and application domain (polymers/materials with specific properties like dielectric constants/PCE).

papers.title: Generative Modeling of Entangled Polymers with a Distance-Based Variational Autoencoder
papers.abstract: We present a variational autoencoder framework for learning and generating configurations of structured polymer globules from distance matrices. We used coarse-grained molecular dynamics to sample polyethylene structures, which we used as the training set for our deep learning model. By combining convolution and attention layers, the model encodes the structural patterns of distance matrices into an organized and roto-translationally invariant latent space of lower dimensionality. The generative capability of the variational autoencoder, coupled with a post-processing pipeline based on multidimensional scaling and short molecular dynamics, enables the recovery of physically meaningful configurations. The reconstructed and generated samples reproduce key observables, including energy, size, and entanglement, despite minor discrepancies in the raw decoder output.
papers.reason: Problem: The paper addresses the problem of generative modeling of polymer configurations from distance matrices using a VAE to learn and generate structures of entangled polyethylene. However, the paper uses distance matrices of polyethylene structures as input and focuses on generating configurations, while the user's idea specifies encoding molecular fingerprints of polymer repeat units and using Bayesian optimization for property search (dielectric constants), which is a different downstream application and input representation. | Domain: Both the user's idea and the paper operate within the domain of polymer science and material design, specifically using variational autoencoders for polymer representation. The paper focuses on 'generative modeling of entangled polymers' and 'learning and generating configurations of structured polymer globules from distance matrices' of polyethylene, while the user's idea aims to 'encode molecular fingerprints of polymer repeat units into a continuous latent space' to 'search for polymers with high dielectric constants'. Both are concerned with encoding polymer information into a latent space for generative or search purposes, indicating a closely related application target within the same sub-area of computational materials science. | Contribution: Both the user's idea and the paper apply a variational autoencoder to polymers to encode molecular information into a continuous latent space. The paper focuses on encoding structural patterns from distance matrices of polyethylene for generating configurations, while the user's idea specifies encoding molecular fingerprints of polymer repeat units and using Bayesian optimization to search for high dielectric constant polymers. The core VAE application to polymers and latent space generation is similar, but the input representation (distance matrices vs. fingerprints), specific polymer properties targeted (structural configurations vs. dielectric constants), and the optimization method (Bayesian optimization vs. direct generation/refinement) are distinct.

papers.title: Polymer informatics at-scale with multitask graph neural networks
papers.abstract: Artificial intelligence-based methods are becoming increasingly effective at screening libraries of polymers down to a selection that is manageable for experimental inquiry. The vast majority of presently adopted approaches for polymer screening rely on handcrafted chemostructural features extracted from polymer repeat units -- a burdensome task as polymer libraries, which approximate the polymer chemical search space, progressively grow over time. Here, we demonstrate that directly "machine-learning" important features from a polymer repeat unit is a cheap and viable alternative to extracting expensive features by hand. Our approach -- based on graph neural networks, multitask learning, and other advanced deep learning techniques -- speeds up feature extraction by one to two orders of magnitude relative to presently adopted handcrafted methods without compromising model accuracy for a variety of polymer property prediction tasks. We anticipate that our approach, which unlocks the screening of truly massive polymer libraries at scale, will enable more sophisticated and large scale screening technologies in the field of polymer informatics.

papers.title: Variational Autoencoder with Learned Latent Structure
papers.abstract: The manifold hypothesis states that high-dimensional data can be modeled as lying on or near a low-dimensional, nonlinear manifold. Variational Autoencoders (VAEs) approximate this manifold by learning mappings from low-dimensional latent vectors to high-dimensional data while encouraging a global structure in the latent space through the use of a specified prior distribution. When this prior does not match the structure of the true data manifold, it can lead to a less accurate model of the data. To resolve this mismatch, we introduce the Variational Autoencoder with Learned Latent Structure (VAELLS) which incorporates a learnable manifold model into the latent space of a VAE. This enables us to learn the nonlinear manifold structure from the data and use that structure to define a prior in the latent space. The integration of a latent manifold model not only ensures that our prior is well-matched to the data, but also allows us to define generative transformation paths in the latent space and describe class manifolds with transformations stemming from examples of each class. We validate our model on examples with known latent structure and also demonstrate its capabilities on a real-world dataset.

The paper's problem is about improving VAEs for general data manifold learning, which is a broader topic than the user's specific application. | Method: The user's idea proposes applying a variational autoencoder (VAE) to encode molecular fingerprints into a continuous latent space and then using Bayesian optimization. The paper's application domains are primarily in computer vision (MNIST digits) and abstract manifold learning (swiss roll, concentric circles). While both use VAEs, the specific data types and application targets (polymers vs. images/abstract data) are different, placing them in the same discipline (machine learning) but different sub-areas or application targets. | Contribution: The paper focuses on improving VAEs by learning latent manifold structures using transport operators to better match data manifolds, define nonlinear paths, and separate classes (VAELLS). The user's idea applies a VAE to molecular fingerprints for polymer design, specifically using Bayesian optimization in the latent space to search for high dielectric constants. While both use VAEs, the paper's contribution is a methodological improvement to VAEs themselves, whereas the user's contribution is an application of VAEs to a specific chemistry problem with an additional optimization component.

papers.title: Shape optimization in laminar flow with a label-guided variational autoencoder
papers.abstract: Computational design optimization in fluid dynamics usually requires to solve non-linear partial differential equations numerically. In this work, we explore a Bayesian optimization approach to minimize an object's drag coefficient in laminar flow based on predicting drag directly from the object shape. Jointly training an architecture combining a variational autoencoder mapping shapes to latent representations and Gaussian process regression allows us to generate improved shapes in the two dimensional case we consider.

Both involve optimizing properties of a physical entity, but the target property and the nature of the entities (shapes vs. molecular fingerprints of polymer repeat units) are distinct. | Method: The paper's methodology is nearly identical to the user's idea. [7] for the design of drug-like molecules, which is directly analogous to the user's proposed application to polymers. | Domain: The user's idea is in materials science/chemistry, specifically polymer design, while the paper is in fluid dynamics, specifically shape optimization for drag reduction. existing_examples.name: Molecular Design using VAEs and Bayesian Optimization
existing_examples.description: Numerous research papers and projects apply VAEs to encode molecular structures (SMILES, fingerprints, graphs) into a latent space, followed by optimization techniques (including Bayesian optimization, genetic algorithms, reinforcement learning) to search for molecules with desired properties (e.g., drug discovery, material design). This approach is a well-established paradigm in cheminformatics and materials informatics.

existing_examples.name: Polymer Informatics for Property Prediction
existing_examples.description: The field of polymer informatics specifically focuses on applying AI/ML techniques to polymer data. This includes encoding polymer structures (repeat units, monomer sequences) and predicting properties like dielectric constant, glass transition temperature, etc. VAEs and other generative models are used to design novel polymers.

existing_examples.name: Inverse Design of Materials
existing_examples.description: The broader field of 'inverse design' aims to find materials with specific target properties. Machine learning, including generative models and optimization algorithms, is a core component of modern inverse design strategies across various material types, including polymers.

matched_sections.heading: Machine Learning Model
matched_sections.text_snippet: Following the approach of the Compact Convolutional Transformer [48, 49], we designed the encoder of our VAE [50] to first transform the input matrix, x , into a sequence of tokens using three 2D convolutional layers with kernel sizes of 7. Finally, the mean and logarithmic variance of the latent representation are computed via two fully-connected layers that map the output of the transformer into two latent vectors, µ and log σ 2 , each of dimension 1200.

matched_sections.heading: INTRODUCTION
matched_sections.text_snippet: In the field of material science, deep generative models have been extensively used to fit interaction potentials for atomistic simulations of solid-state and organic systems [9-11] and have been leveraged to bridge microscopic structures of amorphous systems with their macroscopic and thermodynamic properties [12-16]. Among these amorphous materials, polymer melts represent a broad class of systems with relevance and potential for technological applications because of their desirable properties, such as flexibility, light weight, and strength [17-19].

Polyethylene is one of the simplest possible polymers, consisting of repeating methylene groups, and is relatively inert with no significant polar groups present.

matched_sections.paper_title: Polymer informatics at-scale with multitask graph neural networks
matched_sections.heading: Introduction
matched_sections.text_snippet: An alternative to handcrafting features is 'machine learn' them. One approach is to represent the material as raw text, such as a simplified molecular-input line-entry system (SMILES) 17 or BigSMILES 18 string, and then learn features with a neural network specifically designed for natural language processing. 19 Another promising approach is to represent the material as a graph, and then train a Graph Neural Network (GNN) 20 to learn features.
matched_sections.reason: The paper discusses 'machine learning' features from polymer repeat units, specifically mentioning GNNs for learning features from graph representations. The user's idea uses a variational autoencoder to encode molecular fingerprints into a continuous latent space, which is a method for learning features and creating a continuous representation, similar in spirit to the paper's approach of learning polymer fingerprints.

matched_sections.paper_title: Polymer informatics at-scale with multitask graph neural networks
matched_sections.heading: Summary and Outlook
matched_sections.text_snippet: In summary, we have produced polyGNN-the first-ever protocol that integrates polymer feature learning from SMILES strings and other relevant features, invariant transformations, data augmentation and multitask learning. Through careful comparison, we show that our protocol culminates in ultrafast polymer fingerprinting and accurate property prediction over the most comprehensive array of chemistries and properties studied to date.

matched_sections.heading: Abstract

matched_sections.paper_title: Variational Autoencoder with Learned Latent Structure
matched_sections.heading: INTRODUCTION
matched_sections.text_snippet: In particular, generative models such as variational autoencoders (VAEs) (Kingma and Welling, 2013; Rezende et al., 2014) and generative adversarial networks (Goodfellow et al., 2014) sample from a latent space with a specified prior distribution in order to generate new, realistic samples. VAEs have the additional benefit of an encoder that maps data inputs into a latent space.
matched_sections.reason: The user's idea proposes using a 'variational autoencoder to encode molecular fingerprints... into a continuous latent space', which is a core function of VAEs as described in the paper's introduction.

matched_sections.paper_title: Variational Autoencoder with Learned Latent Structure
matched_sections.heading: Variational Autoencoder
matched_sections.text_snippet: The VAE model learns a low-dimensional latent representation by defining a generator function g : Z → X that maps latent points z ∈ R d to high-dimensional data points x ∈ R D . The desired objective for training a VAE is maximizing the log-likelihood of a dataset X = { x 1 , . . . , x N } given by 1 N log p ( X ) = 1 N ∑ N i =1 log ∫ p ( x i , z ) dz .
matched_sections.reason: The user's idea of using a variational autoencoder to encode data into a latent space is a fundamental aspect of VAEs, as detailed in this section of the paper.

matched_sections.paper_title: Shape optimization in laminar flow with a label-guided variational autoencoder
matched_sections.heading: Introduction
matched_sections.text_snippet: In this work we explore whether Bayesian optimization can facilitate the design process. As an exemplary and general problem we discuss the resistance of an object in a fluid (e.g. air or water). What shape should a vesicle have to reduce energy dissipation by drag to a minimum?

matched_sections.paper_title: Shape optimization in laminar flow with a label-guided variational autoencoder
matched_sections.heading: Abstract
matched_sections.text_snippet: Jointly training an architecture combining a variational autoencoder mapping shapes to latent representations and Gaussian process regression allows us to generate improved shapes in the two dimensional case we consider.
matched_sections.reason: The user's idea proposes using a variational autoencoder to encode into a continuous latent space and then using Bayesian optimization. The paper uses a variational autoencoder mapping shapes to latent representations and Gaussian process regression (a component of Bayesian optimization) to generate improved shapes.

matched_sections.paper_title: Shape optimization in laminar flow with a label-guided variational autoencoder
matched_sections.heading: Guided shape encoding and Bayesian optimization
matched_sections.text_snippet: Object shapes are encoded as binary images of size 112 × 84 and mapped to and from a continuous latent space z of dimensionality d by two neural networks forming the encoder and decoder pair of a variational autoencoder (VAE) [8]. We map the latent feature vectors z to drag coefficient y through a sparse-spectrum Gaussian process (GP) regression model [9]. We choose the sparse approach to avoid the computational complexity of a full GP. Next to the most probable drag coefficient, the Gaussian process provides us also with an uncertainty estimate when predicting the drag of unobserved points in the latent space. The probabilistic measure allows us to calculate expected improvement (EI) [10] for each new point with respect to the smallest drag coefficient observed so far and optimize towards larger EI values using gradient ascent.
matched_sections.reason: The user's idea explicitly mentions 'applying a variational autoencoder to encode... into a continuous latent space' and 'using Bayesian optimization in that space'. The paper details using a VAE to map object shapes to and from a continuous latent space and then using a Gaussian process regression model (a core component of Bayesian optimization) to predict properties and optimize using expected improvement in that latent space.

matched_sections.paper_title: Shape optimization in laminar flow with a label-guided variational autoencoder
matched_sections.heading: Abstract
matched_sections.text_snippet: Computational design optimization in fluid dynamics usually requires to solve non-linear partial differential equations numerically. In this work, we explore a Bayesian optimization approach to minimize an object's drag coefficient in laminar flow based on predicting drag directly from the object shape.
matched_sections.reason: The user's idea is in materials science/chemistry (polymers, molecular fingerprints, dielectric constants), while the paper is in fluid dynamics (object shapes, drag coefficients). These are different application domains.

linked_sections.paper_title: Constrained Bayesian Optimization for Automatic Chemical Design
linked_sections.heading: Abstract
linked_sections.text_snippet: Automatic Chemical Design is a framework for generating novel molecules with optimized properties. The original scheme, featuring Bayesian optimization over the latent space of a variational autoencoder, suffers from the pathology that it tends to produce invalid molecular structures.
